what_it_does: Evaluates LibreChat as a self-hosted chat-UI platform candidate for the Company fabric front-end, documenting architecture, extensibility, auth, licensing, MCP support, maturity, and use-case fit.
observations: Selected as the conventional React/Node candidate over alternatives (AnythingLLM, Jan, Big-AGI).
Live-source grounding from GitHub and librechat.ai, with a correction that MCP docs list three transports (stdio, SSE, streamable-HTTP), not four.
MIT license with no branding or commercial-derivative clause, verified against primary source.
First-class, config-driven MCP support via librechat.yaml; fabric reachable as MCP server or OpenAI-compatible custom endpoint.
No native server-to-browser push; SSE is limited to in-flight LLM token streaming.
Bounded fork estimate (~3–5 new files) to add persistent SSE/WebSocket route and custom React panel.
Enterprise auth includes OIDC, SAML, LDAP/AD, OAuth2, ACL-based RBAC, and per-user MCP isolation.
Installable PWA via vite-plugin-pwa; no native mobile app; mobile optimization depth unverified.
Agents framework includes Code Interpreter, File Search, Actions, Agent Chain, Subagents, Skills, Artifacts.
ClickHouse now backing the project; ~39.6k stars and daily commits as of June 2026 scrape.
standouts: Two zero-code integration paths: custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint and fabric-as-MCP-server.
Conventional React 18 + Express 5 + MongoDB stack described as lowering fork difficulty.
No general-purpose server-to-browser channel; push requires a bounded fork.
Config-driven MCP with streamable-HTTP recommended for production.
conventions: Follows a numbered research-note structure with summary, detailed sections, and bottom line.
Cites live primary sources and includes a correction note for an earlier claim.
Uses a shared rubric and references companion comparison files.
Uses comparative superlatives (e.g., 'most forkable,' 'strongest') even though the ledger intent is neutral.
concerns: High dependence on a single live scrape (June 2026); star/commit counts and backing news may age quickly.
The file admits that mobile optimization depth and some docs paths (branding) are unverified.
Integration for fabric-initiated push requires a fork with ongoing merge cost against daily upstream commits.
'HIGHEST-stakes' claims rely on lead verification, not independently reproducible in the file.
The correction note reveals a prior factual error in the research stream (websocket transport).
notes: A live-verified research note that positions LibreChat as a candidate because of its permissive license and conventional stack, while clearly flagging that fabric-to-UI push needs a bounded fork.
questions: What does the shared rubric in _SHARED-RUBRIC.md actually weigh?
How will the fabric's models and endpoints map to the custom endpoint YAML stanza?
Which transport and panel design would the bounded fork use (SSE vs WebSocket)?
How do per-user MCP isolation and ACL roles align with the fabric's identity model?
What is the acceptable ongoing merge/maintenance cost for the fork?
What concrete governance changes will ClickHouse backing introduce?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file is a focused evaluation of LibreChat against the stated shared rubric and use-case priorities.
apparent_themes: Config-driven integration over code changes where possible.
Live-source verification and explicit correction of errors.
Acknowledgment of real-time push as an integration gap.
intention_signals: To provide a decision-ready candidate assessment that justifies selecting LibreChat because its conventional stack and dual zero-code integration paths make the required fork for server-push feel bounded, while transparently noting the gap.
novelty: The explicit correction of an MCP transport claim and the framing of 'fork tractability' as the primary selection rationale, plus the detailed bounded-fork scope estimate.
contribution: A factual candidate profile for LibreChat, including verified license terms, MCP transport details, integration paths, auth/RBAC depth, PWA status, and a concrete fork-scope estimate for adding server-to-browser push, along with a correction note that helps maintain accuracy in the fused ledger.
summary_for_embedding: This file is a research evaluation of LibreChat as a self-hosted chat-UI platform candidate for the Company fabric front-end, documenting its conventional React/Express/MongoDB architecture, MIT licensing, first-class config-driven MCP support, OpenAI-compatible custom endpoint integration, enterprise auth/RBAC, PWA status, and the need for a bounded fork to add server-to-browser push.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to provide a live-source-backed, decision-ready assessment of LibreChat against the shared rubric and the fabric use-case priorities.
